old_claim.note: Kinship Systems provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Ancestor veneration depends on remembered persons becoming community forebears, so kinship and descent categories supply the social relationship being ritually maintained.

source_shape.source_locator: The British Museum says the Jericho plastered skulls likely became ancestor figures who may have been worshipped and were portraits of community forebears.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source ties plastered skulls to remembered individuals becoming ancestor figures and community forebears.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Ancestor veneration is a social relationship with remembered dead forebears; kinship systems supply the categories that make those forebear relationships meaningful.

ontology_before: Ancestor worship depended on kinship systems through a generic expert-inference edge.
ontology_after: Neolithic ancestor veneration keeps kinship systems as the source-backed social prerequisite for forebear veneration.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: kinship_systems remains the direct social prerequisite, now source-backed.
would_reject_if: The node were broadened back to all ancestor worship traditions worldwide.
A source only supported intentional burial without identifying ancestor figures or community forebears.
Kinship systems were treated as a hard prerequisite for every mortuary ritual rather than enabling infrastructure for this ancestor-veneration scope.
short_reason: Jericho plastered-skull evidence supports ancestor figures and community forebears, making kinship the direct social prerequisite.
